text:
Paris 2024 Olympics organisers have apologised after a scene from the opening ceremony depicting religious imagery sparked controversy among commentators and the Catholic Church.

Paris 2024 Summer Olympics organisers have issued an apology following backlash over religious depictions during the opening ceremony on Friday, with commentators pointing out the hypocrisy of those behind the stunt.

A tableau entitled “Festivity” drew criticism for appearing to depict Leonardo da Vinci’s painting of The Last Supper, showing Jesus Christ with his apostles.

The controversial segment of the opening ceremony featured singer Philippe Katerine painted blue and almost naked while surrounded by drag queens and models.

After condemnation from politicians and religious groups, including the Catholic Church in France, organisers of the ceremony apologised for causing offence.

The interpretation of the Greek God Dionysus makes us aware of the absurdity of violence between human beings.#Paris2024#OpeningCeremonypic.twitter.com/FBlQNNUmvV

Sky News journalist Caroline Marcus said while no religion was above scrutiny, the stunt exposed the glaring hypocrisy of so-called progressives, who would never dare to satirise Islam.

"No religion or ideology should be above mockery or satire ... it is the inconsistency which seems to be applied," she said on Sunday Showdown.

"Have some courage, try it with Islam, what would happen? You would have a fatwa slapped on your head faster than Ariarne Titmus can swim the 400 metre freestyle."

Paris 2024 spokeswoman Anne Descamps said: “It is clear that there was never any intention to show disrespect to any religious group”

Speaking at a press conference on Sunday, Ms Descamps added: “I think (with artistic director) Thomas Jolly, we really did try to celebrate community tolerance.”

“Looking at the result of the polls that we shared, we believe that this ambition was achieved. If people have taken any offence we are, of course, really, really sorry.”

Mr Jolly, however, defended the flamboyant scene and told French media: “You will never find in my work a desire to denigrate anyone or anything.”

Commentators have broadly condemned the “woke” opening ceremony with scenes that “seek to ridicule Christians”.

The scene was harshly criticised by French politicians as well as the French episcopal conference.

French politician Marion Maréchal described the ceremony as “crude woke propaganda” and said the event was taken “ideologically hostage” by left-wing activists.

The Catholic Church of France also expressed its disapproval and said: "Unfortunately, this ceremony contained scenes that mocked Christianity, which we deeply regret."

Although, the statement added there were also “wonderful moments of beauty and joy, rich in emotion and universally recognised”.

French President Emmanuel Macron and his government have celebrated the event’s success as more than 80 per cent of France held positive views about the ceremony.

“Thanks to Thomas Jolly and his creative genius for this grandiose ceremony,” President Macron wrote on X.

“Thank you to the artists for this unique and magical moment… We'll be talking about it in 100 years' time!”
